itineraries.title: Lithuania Discovery: Baroque Vilnius, Hill of Crosses and Curonian Spit

map_keywords: Palace of the Grand Dukes Vilnius

visits.text: Jour 1: Explore Vilnius Historic Center, a UNESCO World Heritage Site that has preserved its remarkable medieval layout despite centuries of conflict and destruction. This atmospheric Old Town showcases an impressive architectural tapestry blending Gothic, Baroque, Renaissance, and classical styles across its winding cobblestone streets. The concentration of historic buildings within such a compact area makes Vilnius one of the best-preserved medieval urban centers in Eastern Europe.

Jour 1: Marvel at the stunning St. Anne's Church, a Gothic masterpiece of red brick architecture that has stood for over 500 years. Adjacent stands the St. Francis of Assisi (Bernardine) Church, creating an impressive ecclesiastical ensemble. Legend holds that Napoleon was so enchanted by St. Anne's beauty that he wished to carry it back to Paris in the palm of his hand.

Jour 1: Pass through the Gate of Dawn, the only surviving gate of the original city wall and home to a revered icon of the Virgin Mary that draws pilgrims from across the Catholic world. This 16th-century gate once formed part of the city's defensive fortifications and now stands as both a religious shrine and architectural landmark. The chapel above the gate houses the miraculous painting venerated for centuries.

Jour 1: Wander along Pilies Street, the main artery of Vilnius Old Town connecting the Cathedral to the Gate of Dawn through the historic heart of the city. This vibrant pedestrian thoroughfare is lined with baroque buildings housing cafés, galleries, and shops selling amber and Lithuanian crafts. Street musicians and outdoor art exhibitions bring this ancient route to life throughout the year.

Jour 1: Discover the Palace of the Grand Dukes of Lithuania, an exceptional museum housed within the meticulously restored 15th-century royal residence at Cathedral Square. The underground ruins of the original palace provide a fascinating archaeological journey, while upper floors display authentic furniture, tapestries, and paintings from Lithuania's golden age. The museum's cutting-edge 3D virtual reality experience transports visitors through centuries of Vilnius history in breathtaking detail.

Jour 1: Ascend to Gediminas Castle Tower, one of Lithuania's most treasured national symbols and the remaining feature of the former Upper Castle. Grand Duke Gediminas established the first wooden fortifications here in the 14th century, founding what would become the Lithuanian capital. According to beloved legend, Gediminas dreamed of an iron wolf howling from this hilltop, which a priest interpreted as a prophecy of a great city whose fame would spread throughout the world.

Jour 1: Admire the grand Vilnius Cathedral and its distinctive Bell Tower standing proudly in Cathedral Square at the heart of the city. This neoclassical cathedral, rebuilt multiple times over seven centuries, serves as the principal Roman Catholic church of Lithuania. The freestanding Bell Tower, once part of the city's fortifications, has become an iconic landmark visible across the Old Town.

Jour 1: Explore the historic Vilnius University complex, one of the oldest and most renowned universities in Eastern Europe, founded in 1579. The university's thirteen interconnected courtyards showcase architectural styles spanning four centuries, from Gothic to Baroque. The magnificent Church of St. Johns within the complex features a stunning Baroque interior and a tower offering panoramic city views.

Jour 2: Visit Trakai Island Castle, a magnificently restored 14th-15th century fortress rising from a small island in the crystal-clear waters of Lake Galvė. This red-brick Gothic castle, surrounded by an archipelago of 21 islands, stands as one of the most photographed landmarks in Lithuania and a powerful symbol of the nation's medieval grandeur. During the era of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, when the realm stretched from the Baltic to the Black Sea, Trakai served as one of the main political centers.

activities.text: Jour 1: Join a walking or cycling tour with local guides to uncover hidden stories and fascinating history beyond the main tourist sites. Expert guides reveal the layers of Vilnius history, from medieval grand dukes to Soviet occupation and the singing revolution that led to independence. These tours provide invaluable context that transforms a simple sightseeing walk into a deeper understanding of Lithuanian culture and resilience.

Jour 2: Rent a rowing boat or kayak to explore the stunning lake landscape surrounding Trakai Castle from the water. The area encompasses over 30 lakes connected by channels, creating a unique aquatic environment perfect for peaceful paddling. Gliding across Lake Galvė offers the most spectacular perspectives of the castle rising from its island setting.

Jour 2: Soar above Trakai in a hot air balloon for an unforgettable aerial perspective of the castle, lakes, and surrounding forests. From above, the full beauty of this lake district reveals itself as a patchwork of water, islands, and forests stretching to the horizon. This magical experience provides photographs and memories that last a lifetime.

visits.text: Discover Kaunas, Lithuania's second-largest city set dramatically at the confluence of the nation's two mightiest rivers, the Nemunas and Neris. This historic city dating back to the 14th century served as Lithuania's temporary capital during the interwar period when Vilnius was under Polish control. Recently designated as a European Capital of Culture, Kaunas has undergone a remarkable renaissance that continues to transform its cultural landscape.

Explore Kaunas Old Town, a compact historic quarter where medieval streets open onto charming squares lined with Gothic and Renaissance architecture. The Town Hall Square, dominated by the elegant white 'Swan' Town Hall, has served as the city's heart for centuries. Surrounding streets reveal hidden churches, merchants' houses, and courtyards that whisper stories of the city's prosperous trading past.

Visit Kaunas Castle, one of the earliest stone fortifications in Lithuania, built in the mid-14th century to defend against the Teutonic Knights. Though partially ruined, the surviving tower and walls powerfully evoke the medieval conflicts that shaped Lithuanian history. The castle's strategic position at the river confluence underscores its military importance in defending the realm.

activities.text: Stroll along Laisvės Alėja (Liberty Avenue), one of the longest pedestrian streets in Europe stretching 1.6 kilometers through the heart of modern Kaunas. This elegant tree-lined boulevard connects the Old Town with the New Town, passing historic buildings, shops, and vibrant cafés. The avenue pulses with local life, offering an authentic glimpse into contemporary Lithuanian urban culture.

map_keywords: Pope John Paul II Mass Šiauliai

visits.text: Experience the Hill of Crosses, one of the most extraordinary and spiritually powerful sites in the world, where hundreds of thousands of crosses cover a small hill in the Lithuanian countryside. This remarkable place began in the early 19th century when people placed crosses to commemorate loved ones who died in uprisings against the Russian Tsar. Despite repeated Soviet attempts to destroy it-removing crosses, closing roads, even trying to flood the area-the faithful continued placing crosses under cover of darkness, transforming the hill into an enduring symbol of resistance and faith.

Witness the incredible density and variety of crosses in all shapes and sizes, from tiny crucifixes to elaborate wooden sculptures several meters tall. The site became an important pilgrimage destination after Pope John Paul II celebrated Mass here in 1993, further cementing its significance in Catholic tradition. Walking among the countless crosses, rosaries, and religious statues creates an overwhelming sensory and spiritual experience unlike any other place on earth.

activities.text: Take time to absorb the profound atmosphere of this unique sacred site, where faith and history intertwine in the most tangible way imaginable. Many visitors choose to add their own cross to the collection, continuing a tradition that spans over two centuries. The Hill of Crosses stands as a testament to the indomitable spirit of the Lithuanian people and their unwavering commitment to religious freedom.

visits.text: Explore the Curonian Spit, a UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of Europe's most unique natural phenomena-a narrow sand-dune peninsula stretching 98 kilometers between the Curonian Lagoon and the Baltic Sea. This remarkable landform, ranging from just 400 meters to 4 kilometers in width, supports only a handful of villages surrounded by endless forests and dramatic shifting dunes. The Curonian Spit feels like a place where time has stood still, offering landscapes found nowhere else on the continent.

Climb Parnidis Dune near Nida, one of the most impressive sand formations on the Curonian Spit rising up to 52 meters above sea level. The panoramic views from the summit encompass the vast lagoon, the Baltic Sea, and the forested dunes stretching toward the Russian border. A striking sundial sculpture marks the highest point, creating an iconic landmark against the ever-shifting sands.

Discover the Grey Dunes (also called Dead Dunes or Agilos Dune) in the Naglių Nature Reserve, haunting sand formations that once buried entire villages beneath their advancing masses. Following intensive logging in the 17th and 18th centuries, these dunes began migrating inland, swallowing settlements in their path. Today, continuous stabilization efforts preserve this ever-evolving landscape, where the power of nature remains dramatically visible.

activities.text: Walk through the pristine pine forests that anchor the dunes and provide habitat for diverse wildlife on this fragile peninsula. The contrast between dense woodland and open sand creates a surreal environment that shifts with the seasons and the winds. These forests represent centuries of careful management to prevent the dunes from consuming the entire spit.

Explore the charming fishing village of Nida, the largest settlement on the Lithuanian portion of the Curonian Spit, with its traditional wooden houses and colorful weathervanes. This picturesque village has attracted artists and writers for over a century, including Thomas Mann who built a summer home here. The peaceful atmosphere and unique light quality continue to inspire visitors from around the world.

seo.h1_title: Lithuania Discovery: Baroque Vilnius, Hill of Crosses and Curonian Spit
seo.intro_paragraph: Wander through Vilnius and its baroque old town set on cobblestones, then take the road to Šiauliai and its striking Hill of Crosses. Explore Kaunas and its interwar architecture, before ending at Nida on the Curonian Spit, between shifting dunes and cold Baltic light.
seo.seo_hook: Baroque spires piercing gray skies, crosses covering sacred hills, shifting dunes along the Baltic. Lithuania, layers of faith and beauty.

places_taglines.LT::vilnius: baroque old town cobblestone courtyards unfold
places_taglines.LT::kaunas: interwar architecture art deco boulevards shine
places_taglines.LT::siauliai: hill of crosses devotion wind whispers
places_taglines.LT::nida: curonian spit shifting dunes baltic shore

itineraries.title: The Baltics: From Vilnius to the Dunes and Riga in 14 Days
itineraries.subtitle: From Vilnius's baroque old town to Riga's Art Nouveau facades, via the soaring dunes of the Curonian Spit.
itineraries.notes: Two ferries are required: Klaipeda→Nida and Nida→Klaipeda, with a return by car to Palanga. In July-August, book accommodation in Nida and Palanga well in advance.

visits.text: Inscribed on UNESCO's World Heritage List in 1994, Vilnius Old Town covers 3.5 km² of medieval streets and blends Gothic, Baroque, and Renaissance—all on a street plan that has barely changed in seven centuries. It's the largest surviving baroque old town north of the Alps, and by far the most easterly. Allow half a day to wander its lanes without map or agenda.

Touristy, yes—but St. Anne's Church deserves the stop. This red-brick Gothic gem from the 15th century so captivated Napoleon that, legend has it, he wanted to carry it back to Paris in the palm of his hand. The adjacent Bernardine Church forms a striking ecclesiastical ensemble with it. Best seen early morning for the light on the facade.

A whisper of rosaries, the scent of incense: the Gate of Dawn concentrates all of Lithuania's Catholic devotion within a chapel six meters high. This 16th-century gate is the sole survivor of the nine original gates in the medieval city wall. Come on a Sunday morning to witness the procession of pilgrims.

Walk Pilies Street from north to south: it connects the cathedral to the Gate of Dawn by cutting through the heart of the old town, past baroque cafés and amber stalls. It's Vilnius's liveliest artery—street musicians included. Take time to spot the hidden courtyards behind the portals.

Lithuanians simply call it 'the castle': Gediminas Tower has dominated Vilnius from its hill since the 14th century. Gediminas, legendary founder of the capital, is said to have dreamed here of an iron wolf howling—interpreted as the sign of a great city to be born. The climb is worth it for the 360° view over the baroque rooftops.

activities.text: If it's your first day in Vilnius, join an evening walking tour: local guides reveal the medieval, Soviet, and post-independence layers that the facades don't tell. Count on 2 hours, around €15-20. Book ahead in high season.

practical_info.tip: Several agencies offer French-language tours from Cathedral Square.

visits.text: Excessively baroque or quietly restrained? The Palace of the Grand Dukes of Lithuania plays both hands: the underground ruins of the original 15th-century palace sit alongside upper floors reconstructed with period furniture and tapestries. A virtual reality experience plunges visitors into medieval Vilnius. Allow 1.5 hours, closed Mondays.

Founded in 1579, Vilnius University was shut down by Russian tsars in 1832 and didn't reopen for a century. Its thirteen interconnected courtyards traverse four centuries of architectural styles. St. John's Church within the complex conceals an interior of rare baroque richness—and its tower offers the finest view over the old town's rooftops.

activities.text: Head down to the Užupis quarter, the self-proclaimed 'Artists' Republic' established in 1997 with its own constitution engraved on plaques in twenty languages. This bohemian micro-state of a few streets beside the Vilnia River is home to all the capital's eccentrics. Terrace cafés, ephemeral galleries: plan an hour of aimless wandering.

practical_info.tip: The Užupis 'constitution' is translated into French—look for the golden plaque on Paupio Street.

visits.text: Red brick on green water: Trakai Castle rises from its island in the middle of Lake Galvė like a Gothic apparition. Built by Duke Kęstutis in the late 14th century and completed by Vytautas the Great around 1409, it served as the royal residence and state treasury of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania. After centuries of ruin, restoration in the 1950s-1960s returned its medieval bearing.

activities.text: Rent a kayak from the Karaite village dock to circle the castle from the water: the perspective from the lake is incomparable. Lake Galvė has 20 islands—the full loop by kayak takes 1.5 hours. Avoid July weekends when boats compete for space.

practical_info.tip: Kayak rental at the dock near the main bridge: around €8/hour in 2024.

visits.text: Five mounds in a misty valley: few know that Kernavė, inscribed on UNESCO's list in 2004, concentrates 10,000 years of human occupation on 194 hectares. It's the former political capital of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, abandoned in the 14th century after Teutonic Knight raids and never rebuilt. This sudden disappearance preserved intact this unique archaeological landscape.

In 1920, with Vilnius occupied by Poland, Kaunas became Lithuania's provisional capital. In twenty years, this provincial town sprouted more than 12,000 modernist buildings—Art Deco, neoclassical, functionalist—to the point of being inscribed on UNESCO's list in 2023 as the only European city representing large-scale interwar urbanization. The medieval Old Town and the modernist New Quarter coexist effortlessly.

Kaunasians call it the 'Swan': the white Gothic Town Hall has dominated Town Hall Square for centuries, surrounded by merchants' facades and hidden churches. Kaunas Castle from the 14th century, built to repel the Teutonic Knights, stands two steps away, partly ruined but evocative. Lively square in the evening, markets on weekends.

Provincial yesterday, UNESCO-listed in 2023 today: Kaunas's modernist quarter (Naujamiestis and Žaliakalnis) is an absolute surprise. Buildings from 1919-1939 with sober yet elaborate facades reveal an architectural optimism that Soviet occupation nearly erased. Birutė Hill and its staircases offer a panoramic view over the rooftops.

activities.text: Walk the 1.6 km of car-free Laisvės Avenue: this pedestrian boulevard links the Old Town to the New Quarter along a line of cafés and Lithuanian boutiques. It's the country's longest pedestrian street. Go late afternoon to see local life in full swing.

practical_info.tip: The street becomes festive on summer evenings—free concerts at Vienybės Square.

visits.text: Tens of thousands of crosses in the wind, the rattle of rosaries in the silence of the plains: the Hill of Crosses (Kryžių kalnas) is one of the strangest and most moving sites in the Baltics. The first crosses appeared after the anti-Russian uprising of 1831. The Soviets bulldozed the site three times—and every night, Lithuanians returned to plant new ones. Arrive early morning to avoid the tour buses.

activities.text: Take time to absorb the profound atmosphere of this unique sacred site, where faith and history intertwine in the most tangible way imaginable. Many visitors choose to add their own cross to the collection, perpetuating a tradition spanning more than two centuries. The Hill of Crosses stands as testimony to the indomitable spirit of the Lithuanian people and their unwavering commitment to religious freedom.

visits.text: Founded by the Teutonic Knights in 1252 as Memelburg, Klaipėda was Prussian, French, Swedish, and Soviet before becoming Lithuanian again in 1923. This composite history reads in its half-timbered lanes and Theatre Square, where Richard Wagner lived and worked early in his career. The old town was rebuilt after the 1854 fire on a grid plan—more rectilinear than its Baltic sisters.

activities.text: If weather permits, take the ferry to the peninsula facing the old town: Klaipėda's aquarium sits there in a baroque building by the water, 1.8 km from shore. It's also the embarkation point for the Curonian Spit. The crossing takes 5 minutes and costs a few euros—essential for the next leg of the itinerary.

practical_info.tip: Ferry every 30 minutes or so from the old town terminal; in summer, queues possible on weekends.

visits.text: No other place in Europe resembles the Curonian Spit, inscribed on UNESCO's list in 2000: 98 km of dunes between the Baltic Sea and the Curonian Lagoon, just 400 meters wide at its narrowest point. Legend says a giantess named Neringa formed it while playing on the beach. Reality is stranger still: entire villages were swallowed by the dunes in the 17th-18th centuries.

At 52 meters above sea level, Parnidis Dune dominates both the lagoon and the Baltic Sea—a breathtaking view of both faces. A sandstone sundial marks the summit. The dune has lost nearly 10 meters of height in thirty years: every visitor who climbs displaces sand. The Parnidis educational trail starts from Nida village.

White sand slips underfoot between dense pines and Nordic sky: the village of Nida has attracted artists and writers for more than a century. Thomas Mann spent several summers here in the 1930s in a house that's now a museum. Jean-Paul Sartre made a memorable stop in 1965—a statue commemorates it. Look for the painted wooden weathervanes typical on the roofs.

activities.text: Take the Grey Dunes trail in the heart of the Naglių nature reserve to see the landscapes that swallowed villages. These 'dead' dunes, stabilized by centuries of pine reforestation, create a surreal environment of pale sand and twisted trunks. The Nagliai educational trail is about 4 km—avoid the heat of the day in summer.

practical_info.tip: Free access from Nida; bring water, no shops on the trail.

visits.text: A 19th-century palace in a botanical park to showcase amber: Palanga's Amber Museum is as unusual as it is successful. Housed in the Tiškevičius Manor, it displays more than 4,500 pieces of Baltic gold, including insect inclusions tens of millions of years old. The adjacent park—designed by French landscape architect Édouard André—is worth the visit alone.

Iodized air and pine resin mingled: Palanga and its 470-meter pier extend into a surprisingly calm Baltic Sea. The town is Lithuania's main seaside resort, with 16,000 winter inhabitants and summer crowds. The pedestrian Jono Basanavičiaus Street concentrates amber shops, restaurants, and old wooden villas. Sunset on the pier is mandatory.

At 249 meters wide, the Venta Rapid is Europe's widest waterfall—but only 1.8 meters high. That's Kuldīga's paradox: a cascade that impresses by breadth, not power. In spring, salmon leap upstream; in the 17th century, they were caught in baskets suspended above the current. Free entry, accessible year-round.

Kuldīga looks like a postcard—and it's true. Its leaning wooden cottages from the 1700s, cobbled lanes, and elegant baroque St. Catherine's Church form one of Latvia's best-preserved historic centers. The town prospered under the Duchy of Courland and had no major industry to disfigure it. Avoid July weekends: buses arrive in groups.

activities.text: Push on to the Riezupe sandstone caves for a striking contrast with yesterday's dunes: these tunnels carved in sandstone by natural erosion create a cool, mysterious underground labyrinth. Accessible on foot from the Venta riverbank. Flashlight useful, modest entrance fee.

practical_info.tip: Open May-October; check with Kuldīga tourist office for hours.

visits.text: Founded in the second half of the 13th century, Ventspils Castle of the Livonian Order is one of Latvia's best-preserved medieval fortresses—it has kept its original footprint for 700 years. Fortress, residence, garrison, school, prison: the castle has done it all before becoming the Ventspils Museum in 2001, featuring an award-winning digital exhibition.

Flower beds at every intersection: Ventspils claims the title of Latvia's floral capital and earns it every summer. The city adds floral sculptures and parterres to its promenades from spring onwards. The market square and Skolas Street show the mix of civic pride and historic architecture that distinguishes this port city.

activities.text: Stroll the Blue Flag-certified beach of Ventspils before heading to Jūrmala: the city has invested massively in its coastal amenities and the result is worth seeing. Harbor promenade, monumental 23-ton anchor, little 1916 steam train: Ventspils is also an unexpected playground. Take the tourist train to reach the beach from the center.

practical_info.tip: The little 'Mazbanitis' train links the center to the beach for a few euros—ideal with children.

visits.text: The 'Latvian Riviera' has earned its credentials: Jūrmala offers 33 km of fine sandy beach bordered by dunes and pine forests. Brezhnev and Khrushchev came here to take the waters. The town owes its reputation to its mineral springs and therapeutic muds that have attracted spa-goers since the 19th century. Main beach at Majori, reachable in 25 minutes by train from Riga.

Walk up Jūras Street to see Art Nouveau and romantic wooden villas from the 1880s-1920s parade by: hundreds of listed or registered wooden houses form a unique open-air museum in the Baltics. Carved verandas, turrets, pastel colors—each building is a variation on the same theme of seaside elegance. Most beautiful on weekdays, when cars have disappeared.

activities.text: Venture into Ķemeri National Park, a wetland paradise celebrated for its vast peat bogs, pristine marshes, and remarkable biodiversity. Well-maintained boardwalk trails wind through this unique ecosystem, allowing visitors to explore without disturbing the fragile environment. The park offers exceptional birdwatching opportunities with numerous rare species inhabiting these atmospheric wetlands.

visits.text: The cobblestones of Riga's old town echo beneath Gothic, Baroque, and Neoclassical facades that succeed one another without logic—that's the beauty of the place, UNESCO-listed since 1997. The reconstructed House of the Blackheads presides over Town Hall Square; St. Peter's Church and its soaring spire organize the panorama. Allow half a day for the lanes and a pause in one of the square's many cafés.

More than 800 Art Nouveau facades in Riga's so-called 'Quiet Center'—the largest concentration in Europe. Alberta Street concentrates the most spectacular creations of Mikhail Eisenstein (father of the filmmaker): howling masks, winged women, and giant medallions on seven different buildings. Built between 1901 and 1908, no two are alike. Visit the Art Nouveau Museum at no. 12, closed Mondays.

Riga's Central Market is spectacular: five former zeppelin hangars converted into halls where Riga Black Balsam rubs shoulders with local cheeses and smoked meats. It's one of Europe's largest covered markets and the place where Riga has done its shopping since the 1930s. Arrive before 10 a.m. to see stalls at full capacity and prices before the tourist influx.

activities.text: If you arrive on a weekday, the market is less crowded and vendors more available to explain their products. Taste the local craft beer and grey peas with smoked bacon, a Latvian specialty. The halls generally close at 5 p.m.; best to spend the morning there and devote the afternoon to the Art Nouveau district.

practical_info.tip: Free entry, complicated parking—take the tram from the center.

visits.text: 42 meters of granite, neither toppled nor moved under Soviet occupation: the Freedom Monument (Brīvības piemineklis) is one of the rare statues to have survived the Soviet era in place. Latvians affectionately call it 'Milda.' Erected in 1935, it represents a woman holding three golden stars symbolizing the country's historic regions. Permanent honor guard.

The Latvian Ethnographic Open-Air Museum gathers more than 100 historic buildings—farms, windmills, workshops—transferred from across the country into a forest park on Riga's edge. It's rural Latvian life across several centuries made visible in one walk. Seasonal events with traditional crafts and folk dances make the visit immersive.

activities.text: Take the tram to Mežaparks park to leave the center and see the residential Riga of the 1920s-1930s: Art Nouveau villas and private gardens in a quarter the guides avoid. The park itself overlooks Lake Ķīšezers—good option for a picnic. Return by tram to the center in 20 minutes.

practical_info.tip: Tram no. 11 from the center; validate your ticket on board.

visits.text: Rigans call it 'the cathedral of music': Riga Dome Cathedral houses one of Europe's largest organs, built in 1884 and since restored. This 13th-century Romanesque-Gothic cathedral is the largest in the Baltic States and served as Lutheran church, Soviet warehouse, then concert hall. Organ concerts are held regularly—check the program before you go.

activities.text: Explore the Latvian Ethnographic Open-Air Museum on Riga's outskirts, bringing centuries of Latvian rural life vividly to life. More than 100 historic buildings including traditional farmsteads, workshops, and windmills showcase the country's diverse regional heritage. Seasonal events featuring traditional craft workshops and folk dance performances make it an immersive cultural experience.

visits.text: Versailles on a smaller scale, Rastrelli more restrained: Rundāle Palace, designed by Francesco Bartolomeo Rastrelli (architect of St. Petersburg's Winter Palace), was built in two phases between 1736 and 1768 for Duke Ernst Johann Biron, favorite of Empress Anna Ivanovna. The 10-hectare French garden, with its rose garden and Green Theatre, won the European Garden Award in 2021. About 40 of the 138 rooms are open to the public.

Two rivers converge, whitewashed walls reflect in the water: Bauska Castle occupies a strategic position at the junction of the Mūsa and Mēmele since the 15th century. The Knights of the Livonian Order first built a fortress here, then the Dukes of Courland added a palace with distinctive red tiles. The site mixes medieval ruins and partially restored interiors.

activities.text: Explore the charming hinterland character of Bauska town, where river channels cut through the heart bordered by family gardens and farmland, spanned by fragile suspension bridges. The pretty market square buzzes with local life, offering authentic insight into small-town Latvian existence. This tranquil setting provides perfect contrast to nearby Rundāle Palace's grandeur.

visits.text: Built in 1214 on the orders of Bishop Albert of Riga, Turaida Castle dominates the Gauja valley like a red-brick vessel in the foliage. Restored in the 20th century after a 1776 fire, it's now one of Latvia's most-visited museums. The main tower offers a 360° view over the valley that local guides compare to the Latvian Switzerland.

The sandstone walls of Gutmanis Cave preserve inscriptions carved since the 17th century—hundreds of travelers have left their names in the soft rock. It's one of the largest caverns in the Baltic States. The legend of the 'Rose of Turaida,' a young girl who chose death over abduction, has been attached to this place since the 13th century.

activities.text: Hike in Gauja National Park, Latvia's largest and oldest, created in 1973. The trail linking Sigulda to Turaida (5.4 km one way) follows the river and Devonian sandstone escarpments. In autumn, the forest turns red and gold—the best season for this section. Bobsleigh and luge activities available in winter.

practical_info.tip: Trail maps available at Sigulda tourist office; some sections are slippery after rain.

visits.text: In 1577, 300 besieged defenders blew themselves up with gunpowder rather than surrender to Ivan the Terrible: Cēsis Medieval Castle has a history to match its imposing ruins. Built from the early 13th century, it was for two centuries the residence of the Grand Master of the Livonian Order. Today you visit the towers by candlelight provided at the entrance—a theatrically effective touch.

The cobbled streets of Cēsis and its white-and-yellow painted wooden houses form one of Latvia's best-preserved historic centers. St. John's Church contains the tombs of Knights of the Livonian Order. Rozu Square and May Park below create a tranquil setting to finish the journey before returning to Riga.

activities.text: Discover the reconstructed lake site at Āraiši, a few kilometers from Cēsis: wooden dwellings on stilts by a lake recreate the life of the early Baltic tribes of the Middle Ages. It's one of the most original open-air museums in the region, adjacent to the ruins of a Livonian Order castle. Allow 45 minutes for the visit, open May to October.

practical_info.tip: 5 km south of Cēsis; take the Rīga road then follow signs for 'Āraiši ezerpils.'

seo.h1_title: The Baltics: From Vilnius to the Dunes and Riga in 14 Days
seo.intro_paragraph: From the baroque old town of Vilnius, inscribed on UNESCO's list in 1994, to Riga's 800 Art Nouveau facades, this 14-day circuit traverses Lithuania and Latvia crossing four UNESCO sites, the Curonian Spit with its shifting dunes, the medieval castles of the Livonian Order, and the surprising modernist architecture of Kaunas.
seo.seo_hook: Baroque Vilnius, UNESCO-listed Curonian dunes, Riga's Art Nouveau, and medieval castles: 14 days to traverse the best of the Baltics.
featured_list: Vilnius: largest baroque old town north of the Alps, UNESCO 1994
Curonian Spit: 98 km of dunes between Baltic and lagoon, UNESCO 2000
Kaunas: modernist capital of the 1920s-1930s, UNESCO 2023
Riga: more than 800 Art Nouveau buildings, record concentration in Europe

places_taglines.LT::vilnius: Where baroque spires pierce the sky above Europe's largest medieval old town.
places_taglines.LT::trakai: A Gothic island fortress rising from mirror-still waters where dukes once ruled.
places_taglines.LT::kernave: Five silent mounds guarding ten millennia of stories beneath their grassy slopes.
places_taglines.LT::kaunas: Where interwar dreams took architectural form across twelve thousand modernist buildings.
places_taglines.LT::klaipeda: Half-timbered lanes whisper of Teutonic knights and the gateway to shifting sands.
places_taglines.LT::nida: Where Thomas Mann found inspiration among dunes that swallowed entire villages.
places_taglines.LT::palanga: Amber washes ashore where pine forests meet the Baltic's endless horizon.
places_taglines.LV::kuldiga: Europe's widest waterfall cascades past wooden cottages frozen in the 1700s.
places_taglines.LV::ventspils: Where medieval fortress walls rise amid seas of summer flowers.
places_taglines.LV::jurmala: Thirty-three kilometers of white sand embraced by Art Nouveau wooden palaces.
places_taglines.LV::riga: Eight hundred facades of sculpted fantasy crown the Baltic's Art Nouveau throne.
places_taglines.LV::rundale: Rastrelli's Baltic masterpiece where Versailles elegance meets Courland ambition.
places_taglines.LV::bauska: Twin rivers converge beneath red-tiled towers where knights once held the frontier.
places_taglines.LV::sigulda: Crimson battlements crown green valleys that earned the name Latvian Switzerland.
places_taglines.LV::cesis: Where three hundred souls chose gunpowder over surrender to the Terrible Tsar.
places_taglines.LT::siauliai: Tens of thousands of crosses rise in defiance where faith outlasted bulldozers.

highlights: Vilnius: the largest baroque old town north of the Alps, UNESCO 1994
Curonian Spit: 98 km of dunes between Baltic Sea and lagoon, UNESCO 2000
Riga: more than 800 Art Nouveau facades, the largest concentration in Europe
